Collocates descriptors for processes that alter the condition of an object or structure; some descriptors collocated here are both such a process and the resultant effect on the item.
Transmission of energy, in the form of heat, sound, or electricity, through a conductor.

A variant of XRF imaging that is based on the use of two x-ray lenses in a confocal configuration, enabling measurements of high spatial resolution in three directions, including depth (x, y, and z) in the micrometer range.

A fluorescence microscopy technique that obtains higher resolution images of very small objects than conventional optical microscopy. Rather than flooding the entire specimen evenly with light, which causes simultaneous excitation of all parts and results in a large out-of focus background part captured by the microscope's photodetector, confocal microscopy uses a condenser lens to focus the light  from a pin-hole onto a very small spot on the plane of the specimen. An optical system rapidly scans the object in synchrony with the illumination. The reflected and emitted rays entering the collector lens are focused onto a plate which generates a signal that forms a display of the image.

Refers to a system of projection by which the points on a sphere are mapped onto a plane, maintaining a one-to-one correspondence of points. It is generally used for map-making, and tries to resolve the problem that arises because a sphere cannot be represented on a flat surface with the preservation of the proportion of all distances. Conformal projection attempts to preserve the relative shape of configurations that surround each point; that is, it preserves the magnitude and sense of angles at each point. Mercator projection and streographic projection are examples of conformal projection.

Refers to a system of projection generally used in map-making and based on the principle of a hollow cone being placed over a sphere so that when the cone is unrolled the line of tangency becomes the central or standard parallel of the area being mapped. In this system, all parallels of latitude are arcs of concentric circles and the meridians of longitude are straight lines generated from the cone's vertex to the divisions of the standard parallel. It is typically derived from a projection of the globe on a cone drawn with a point above one of the poles and tangent to the Earth or other heavenly body at one selected parallel. It may also be derived from a projection where the cone is arranged to intersect the sphere at two closely spaced parallels.

The action or process of conserving for the preservation of existence, state, life, health, safeguarding from destructive influences, natural decay, or waste. May apply to conserving a building, object, environment, species, or other thing.
Stabilizing degraded or weakened areas by introducing or attaching materials capable of holding them together.
The process of building by combining parts or elements, and also for the manner in which the buiilt work has been put together.
General term for processes and techniques used for construction and assembling.

Making photographic prints by placing a negative in contact with sensitized paper and exposing the sandwich to strong light, giving a print the same size as the negative. Many 19th-century printing processes employed contact printing because they required exposure to intense light.

An industrial process in which molten metal is solidified into a continuous length for further processing, rather than being formed into individual ingots. Molten metal is forced through a water-cooled die to create a two-dimensional profile of indeterminate length. The resulting material may then be cut into shorter lengths.

Line drawing technique, often used as a training exercise, where the artist or student draws an outline of a subject without lifting their pencil from paper, and without glancing down at the drawing. The method was popularized by Kimon Nicolaïdes in his book The Natural Way to Draw (1941).
Refers to a weathering phenomenon commonly found on rock surfaces in salt-rich environments whereby cracks follow the surface detailing of a stone.
The action or process of becoming smaller, shorter, or pressed together.

Photogrammetry technique that uses multiple images taken at converging angles with a number of reference points common to all of the images. May be further processed using specialized image processing software to create a three-dimensional image.
Proccess of removing heat in order to lower the temperature.
The construction and repair of wooden vessels formed of staves and hoops, such as casks, buckets, barrels, and tubs.
Refers to the process of engraving for printing using copper plates. It was replaced in the early 19th century by the use of more durable plates, generally made of either steel or steel-faced copper.
Activity of working with copper, especially to create objects such as kettles, coils, tubing, and fittings.
Refers to any of various processes or techniques involving making, imitating, or reproducing objects, built works, or documents.
A ceramic technique in which fine cord is wrapped around a paddle and pressed against an unfired clay vessel, leaving the cord imprint.

Quilting technique in which two parallel lines of stitching are made through two layers of cloth, then heavy yarn or soft cord is inserted between the layers within the lines of stitching, making a raised linear pattern on an otherwise flat ground.
Process of manufacturing glass vessels around a core of material made in the shape of the desired vessel.
Hairstyling process in which hair is parted into rows and braided firmly on the surface of the scalp into various patterns.

Refers to a type of lacquerware made in central and northern China from the 17th century and particularly popular in the 18th century in European high society. Coromandel lacquerware is characterized by its incised decorations filled with brightly colored lacquer or oil paint. The name comes from a part of the southeastern Indian coast near Madras that was a transfer point for Far Eastern goods being shipped to Europe by England's East India Company. The most well-known Coromandel lacquerware are the large Coromandel screens. The term is sometimes used to refer to the technique of doing the lacquering. The Japanese were also familiar with the technique but rarely used it for their lacquerware.

The deterioration of metal or other substances by chemical or electrochemical reaction resulting from exposure to weathering, moisture, chemicals, or other agents, causing the metal to change from a metallic state into a chemically more stable mineral compound known as a corrosion product.
Drawing or bending into folds or alternate furrows and ridges.

Distinctive type of opus sectile ("cut work") technique, by which small triangles and rectangles of colored stones and glass mosaics are set into stone matrices or encrusted upon stone surfaces of floors, walls, or church fixtures; particularly as used in works with geometric patterns produced in Rome from the 12th to the 14th centuries. The name derives from the surname of a family of marbleworkers active in Rome, although all works of this type were not made by this one family.
Embroidering a design by laying down a thread and fastening it with small stitches at regular intervals.
Refers to the pressing of a wet sheet of newly made paper still on the mold onto a felt.
The process of making a counterproof, which is printing a mirror-image from an original printed or drawn work, including both art works and printed or handwritten materials.
Enumerating individual units out of a body of units to determine the total number of units concerned.
Technique of constructing masonry walling made of stones laid up in regular horizontal rows or courses.

Animation technique that implements a courvoisier set-up, or grouping, in which figures are trimmed to their edges, glued to a background typically composed of wood veneer, patterns, or hand-painted watercolors, and covered by a protective top cel. The technique was developed by the Courvoisier Galleries in the late 1930s and early 1940s.
Fracturing in a material or object, usually along a single or branched path.
The severing of a glass object from the blowpipe or pontil rod.
Developing a network of fine cracks in a coating layer, such as a ground, paint layer, varnish, or glaze.
The technique of reinforcing with a framework, for example in painting conservation to reinforce wooden panels, or in architecture to support an arched or coved ceiling.
Activity that consists of producing (often) decorative works with various materials. This is often encouraged from an early age to spark creativity in children.

18th-century printmaking process that imitates the appearance of chalk lines; employs etching, with the ground having been marked with a roulette, and sometimes the addition of directly engraved stippling.
The process of creating a crease or creases.
Technique of submerging a printing plate in acid in stages to produce subtle tonal gradations or other effects.

The process of making small pleats or folds in paper with a blade, a process which imparts stretch, strength, bulk, conformability, and texture similar to that found in fabrics. It also refers to the crimping or frizzing of hair or a similar treatment for rubber or thin fabric.
Pinching together, often to make wavy or bent; also the process of drawing molten glass to form the neck of a vessel.
Textile construction involving the interlocking of looped stitches, employing a single cord or strand of yarn and a single hooked needle.

The trimming or cutting away of edges, as portions of a photograph or printed page. To trim; cutting down prints, photographs, or pages to a required size, often without regard for their original composition.
In film photography, the use of chemicals intended for developing one kind of film (e.g. color) and applying them to another (e.g. negative), and vice versa.
Drawing technique in which lines move in any direction across the form rather than along the edge of it.
Inferring the date of one thing, as an artifact, a pollen deposit, or a tree ring, from the age of a similar one that has been more firmly dated.
Hatching in which a network of lines creating a darker value is made by drawing one set of hatchings over another at a different angle.

Refers to the process of using thread to create two stitches of the same length that cross each other to form crosses or "x" shapes. It is often used to make designs on fine fabrics or heavy canvas or carpet. It is also used to secure seams or splices in pieces of fabric.
The process of writing in overlapping layers at right or multiple angles on a support. A practice that became prevalent in nineteenth century correspondence to save on postage priced by weight.
Joining, by comparatively short connecting units, neighboring chains of atoms in a complex chemical molecule.
Pressing or twisting into a compact mass of irregular folds or wrinkles.
The action of compressing something from its original shape in order to break, reduce, or destroy it.
The action of forming crystals, or a crystalline structure, through a purification process which takes place in many substances while cooling from a state of fusion or solution.

A technique of decoration employed in Spain for tiles and other wares which superseded the cuerda seca method. It involved impressing the pattern on tiles to form ridges which prevented the colored glazes from intermingling.

Decoration technique in which outlines are drawn on the surface of wares (especially tiles) with a greasy substance colored with manganese, allowing glazes of several colors to be used without intermingling; the grease disappears during firing; found primarily in Iberia.
Process of refining precious metals like gold and silver; the impurities are oxidized and partly absorbed into the cupel.
Formation of a concavity or cuplike hollow in a paint layer.

Generally, any process or method involving aging, seasoning, washing, drying, heating, smoking, or otherwise treating whereby a product is preserved, perfected, or readied for use. In the context of leather manufacture, use specifically for processes such as "brining", "dry-salting", and "wet-salting" done to prevent putrifaction or other damage to hides and skins before tanning.
The distortion of an object originally linear or planar so that it is curved or coiled in shape.

Incorporating a fatty substance into heavy leather, such as belting leather, to increase its strength, pliability, and water repellency; distinguished from "fat-liquoring," which is the introduction of a fatty substance into light leather.

Refers to a perspective system derived from linear perspective, but employing curved lines to account for the optical illusion that wide or tall forms at right angles to the viewer's line of sight undergo perspectival diminution towards their extreme edges. In this system, a wide wall parallel to the picture plane would appear to taper at the left and right. The system was discussed by Leonardo da Vinci in the Renaissance, but was not often used until the 19th and 20th centuries.

Process where the owner or inhabitant may make various choices in the final design of the home, based on a general model or set of possibilities that are provided. Variations of a custom build include homes on a serviced plot, shells, and turnkey.
Making or modifying something according to individual specifications or preferences.

Type of animation in which cutout pieces of artwork made of paper, wood, cardboard, metal or other material are laid out on a background under an animation camera, moved by hand, and shot incrementally to create the illusion of motion when the film or video is projected at normal speed.
The process of shaping glass, making facets, grooves, and depressions, by grinding using rotating disks of various materials, sizes, and shapes and a stream of water with an abrasive.
Making incisions with a sharp-edged instrument, thus removing material or dividing something into parts.

A traditional jewelry casting technique. The elliptical back plate or cuttlebone of the ordinary squid serves as the mold material. The desired pattern is carved into one half of the divided cuttlebone. The two halves are attached and heated metal is poured through a rounded channel cut into the top of the bone.

Refers to a system of projection used to transfer information from a globe to a flat surface. It is generally used in cartography where a portion of the Earth or other heavenly body is represented as if it were transferred to the surface of a cylinder and then unrolled as a plane on which parallels of latitude are horizontal lines and meridians of longitude appear as vertical lines.
The method or technique of analyzing and identifying fingerprints. For the branch of forensic science that studies fingerprints, use "dactylography."

Process that produces a direct positive image on a silver-coated copper plate, invented by Louis-Jacques-Mandé Daguerre of France and Joseph-Nicéphore Niepce in the 1830s. In the process, a copper plate is coated with silver iodide and exposed to light in a camera, then fumed with mercury vapour and fixed with common salt in solution.

Sculpture construction technique referred to as hollow dry lacquer where layers of lacquer soaked cloth are applied over a clay mold. Once the lacquer dries, the mold is removed and a wooden armature is sometimes inserted to prevent warping. This technique was popular during the Hakuhou (645-710) and Nara (710-749) periods and was largely replaced by the wood-core dry lacquer technique, "mokushin kanshitsu," by the end of the Nara period.
The process or activity of hurting, injuring, or otherwise causing damage, often in a way results in the lessening or loss of value of a thing.
Decorating metal, usually iron, with flat ornaments of precious metal, such as gold or silver.
Refers to the dissipation of energy in motion of any type, especially oscillatory motion, and the consequent reduction or decay of the motion.

Traditional Korean decorative coloring done on wooden buildings and structures, such as royal palaces and temples. Its usual function was to cover design defects and prevent cracks in wooden buildings, in addition to repelling pests and protecting from rain and wind. Since dancheong is based on yin and yang and the Five Elements Theory, blue is placed to the east, red south, yellow center, white west and black north, in addition to green. In South Korea, the color pigments with specific color index numbers are standardized by the state.
To make or become dark in shade, value, or color; a form of discoloration: the blackening or browning of materials due to such causes as chemical process, exposure to light, the passage of  time.

The technique of visually representing sets of facts or statistics collected for reference and analysis in graphical or schematic form. Formats used in data visualization typically include tables, charts and graphs, but may take a variety of pictographic forms. Data visualization increasingly involves digital rendering or processes, but pre- and co-exists with purely analog means of representation.

The process or activity of writing, marking, or delineating a date on something, usually indicating when the thing was created, and usually with the intention that the inscription will be conspicuous or durable.
Determining or fixing a date of origination, fabrication, composition, or occurrence.
The removal or reduction of acid content by neutralizing the acidic components in a material, often paper, and providing an alkaline buffer to counteract acidic build-up in the future.

Process like that of embossing, except inverted; for instance, where a relief plate is placed under a sheet of paper as it runs through a press, lowering areas of the paper as opposed to raising them.
Process of removing calcium or calcium compounds from a calcified material such as bone, soil, mortar.

Technique of transferring pictures and designs from specially prepared paper onto a surface, such as glass, and the Surrealist painting technique in which one freshly painted or inked surface is pressed against another to form an image.
In metallurgy, removal of carbon content from metal, usually steel.
Removal of chlorine from water or another substance.

The tearing away of parts of posters, or other materials, that have been applied in layers, so that selected portions of the underlayers contribute to the total image. To designate the resulting works, use "affiches lacerérées."

Removal or deprivation of color. In discussion of textiles, decolorizing refers to removal of a dyestuff, whether by physical separation methods such as washing, or by chemical means such as bleaching.
Dissolution into separate parts, used especially of organic materials or of such changes at a molecular level.

Process of dismantling buildings or other large constructions in a way that allows the reuse of materials and minimum negative impact on the surrounding structures or environment. It prioritizes salvaging materials for reuse or recycling. It is in contrast to the traditional demolition processes that use heavy machinery to raze structures and dump the debris in landfills.
Removal of contaminants such as radioactive material or dangerous gases from areas, objects, living things.

The process of embellishing architecture, furniture, or other objects. In reference to specific forms of decoration, such as fluting, finials, or monograms, that embellish and are part of the building or object, but are not structurally essential to it, use "ornaments"
The technique of decorating surfaces with cut-outs of paper, linoleum, plastic, or other flat material, over which a finish is applied.
Form of buttoning originating in the Victorian period, producing deeply puckered, comfortable upholstery.
In Chinese landscape painting, perspective created through a view past high mountains into the distance.
The biting of a printing plate to such a degree that the printing area becomes substantially recessed and thereby productive of sharper definition and longer runs.
The act or result of intentionally inflicting damage on the surface or outer appearance of buildings or objects.
The process of dispersing particles and breaking up lumps within a substance in order to create greater fluidity; frequently used in ceramics to convert clay into slip.
Permanent removal of forests and woodland.
Term refers to the process of demagnetization, originally used in the context of warships, now extended to magnetic media such as audio tapes and computer storage media.
Gradual loss of quality; can be without structural change.
Removal of fat or grease from material by various means. In a conservation context, the concept chiefly refers to the cleaning of skeletons and skins.
Removal of gum or gummy substance from a material, deglutination. In textile conservation, refers specifically to removal of sericin from  raw silk by various methods.
Removal of water vapor from air by chemical or physical methods.
The act or process of removing or depriving of water; though other liquids may remain.  For the process of making free, or nearly free, of liquids, use "drying."
The removal of ions from a solution by ion exchange.
Separation into constituent layers.
The removal of lignin from woody tissue, usually by industrial chemical processes. Delignification is undertaken in the pulping of wood for applications such as paper-making.
The process in which a crystalline solid absorbs atmospheric water vapor until the crystal eventually melts or liquefies into a saturated solution.
The removal of minerals, usually from water.
Refers to the action of physically destroying or deconstructing something; particularly aplied to a building or other structure that is pulled or knocked down, reduced to rubble or ruins.

In biology, a modification of a protein’s structure brought about by heat, alkalis, and other factors, which causes it to collapse into a disordered mass. The process Is irreversible for most proteins. In conservation science, the denaturation of leather and parchment is studied by various analytical techniques.

A technique that has been used for species identification and quantitation of yeast populations in foods and beverages. In a conservation context, it is used to identify biodeteriogens on cultural heritage assets such as wall paintings and textiles, as well as paper and stone.

Refers to partial heat denaturation of DNA strands performed on a high-performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC). It uses heteroduplex formation between wild-type and mutated DNA strands to identify mutations.
The technique of dating events, intervals of time, and variations in environment understood by studying the sequence of and differences between rings of growth in trees and aged wood.
Measurement of the intensity of transmitted or reflected light by means of a densitometer in order to measure the optical density of a substance or object.
The process or condition of making or having hollows or impressions in a surface, such as by a blow with a sharp or edged instrument.

The removal, by chemical or mechanical means, of epidermal structures such as hair from flayed skin or hide; also serves to loosen and expand dermal fiber network and alters the swelling characteristics of the hide or skin.

Subtractive process which progressively removes base metals from a gold-containing alloy to produce a nearly pure layer of gold. Depletion gilding was used in ancient Greece, in Egypt in the second century BC, and in pre-Columbian America.
The action or process by which a polymer is decomposed into its constituent monomers.
Structural examination with the aim of obtaining information on the variation of composition with depth below the initial surface.
The process of removing historical restorations. A common reason is that the old restorations are now considered bad practice.
Removal of salts, as from water or soil.
Removal of sizing from textiles.

The process in which a material leaves the surface of the solid into which it was absorbed or adsorbed; it is the reverse of those processes. In conservation, adsorption may refer as well to intentional removal of absorbed or adsorbed material from a substrate through increased heat, decreased pressure, or other measures.

Process of ruining, demolishing, pulling to pieces, reducing to fragments, undoing, wasting, rendering useless, putting an end to a material object, structure, or other thing. May also refer to the ruining of an immaterial thing.
The process of freeing a material or object from infusion of sulphur.

The removal of an object or object component from physical contact with another. For the dividing of a whole object or aggregate of objects or object components into constituent parts, see "separating."
Broadly, the action or process of growing worse or becoming impaired in quality, state, or condition.
The act of removing the poison or effect of poison from something.
Procedures for making a latent photographic image visible and then fixing it (making it insensitive to further changes from light).
In glass, the loss of transparency caused by crystallization or deposition of one or more of its ingredients.
A pattern formed on a fabric, especially velvet, by burning the pile away with acid.
Refers to the removal of water from a site to maintain a dry and stable condition during construction.

A spontaneous phenomenon where a thin film on a surface ruptures into an ensemble of separated objects, like droplets, stripes, or pillars. This occurs when a liquid film retracts from a solid to form a bead-shaped drop.

Distinctive type of ancient lost-wax casting technique named after the Dhokra tribes, who are the traditional metalworkers of West Bengal. It is distinguished by decoration made using a pipette to squeeze out long strings of wax that are applied as coils, spirals, strips, or other designs to the mold. Among the items produced by this technique are folk deity figures, votive horsemen, lamps, dowry rice bowls, animal figures, and fish-shaped openwork brass purses.
The physical and chemical changes occurring in sediments between the times of deposition and solidification.
Processes in which particles are selectively removed from a liquid by means of their unequal diffusion through membranes.
Operation in which a rough diamond is cut into a faceted stone that can be used in jewelry.
Pleated tufting done in a diamond pattern.
A technique for decorating glass, and sometimes porcelain, by scratching or stippling it with a diamond-tipped stylus.
Printing process based on light-sensitive diazonium compounds, most often used to reproduce technical drawings.
Metal casting technique in which a molten metal (as a zinc, lead, or aluminum alloy) is forced into a die.
Cutting, as metal or paper, with dies.
Shaping material, such as paper or sheet metal, between two plates, one with the design in relief and the other with the corresponding design recessed.
Analytical methods based on the measurement of the dielectric constant of a substance resulting from the orientation of particles (molecules or ions) that have a dipole moment in an electric field.

Analytical technique used to measure changes in energy of a material as a sample and a reference are individually heated at a predetermined rate. Heat-generating or absorbing processes are detected as differences in electrical energy supplied to either the sample or the reference material. The heat flow into or out of the sample is due to the occurrence of a physical or chemical process  such as melting, crystallization, or ceramic firing temperature.

A technique for observing the temperature, direction, and magnitude of thermally induced transitions in a material by heating or cooling a sample and comparing its temperature with that of an inert reference material under similar conditions.
The science that measures diffraction by matter of x-rays, neutrons, or electrons to study crystal atomic structure; the use of a diffractometer.

Process whereby particles intermingle and move from a region of higher to one of lower concentration, and for the transmission or reflection of light. Includes the effect of such processes, such as the softening of sharp outlines in a photograph.

Photographic process used primarily for instant or self-developing photographs in which the positive image is formed from undeveloped silver halides in the negative. The negative is placed in contact with a support in the presence of a developing agent, and the unexposed silver diffuses to the support to form the positive image. For making color instant camera photographs, it is dye couplers that are in the negative, in which case use "dye diffusion transfer process" or "internal dye diffusion transfer process."
Copying process employing silver halides in which both a positive and a negative copy are produced simultaneously.

Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is an optical image analysis method, based on gray value digital images captured by an optical sensor at a rate of 1,500 images per second. It employs tracking and image registration techniques to measure deformation, displacement, and strain on almost any material.
The recording, storage, and manipulation of images in computer systems in the form of electronically encoded picture elements.

The technique or process of using microscopes that have a digital camera attached instead of an eyepiece and display the digitally acquired image on a large monitor. This technique may include functions such as dimension measurement, and recording of still images and moving images.
Photogrammetry performed using digital image capture.
Photography using digital cameras, which are cameras that acquire images via an electronic sensor and storing them as digital information on a memory device.

Filmless x-ray examination technique which produces images that can be stored as a computer file. Sensed analog signals representing intensity of x-ray photons at all points are converted to digital signals via laser scanning of the image receptor.

Construction of digital, virtual objects or structures based on historic, archaeological, or other similar evidence. Includes manipulation via software of digital copies of damaged objects to effect a virtual restoration.
The filling in of losses and lacunae in the course of virtual anastylosis and virtual restoration.
Computer software technique for rendering virtual models of surfaces in three-dimensions.
Computer software technique for rendering virtual models of three-dimensional terrain.
Writing that, by means of various electronic, computerized devices, results in digital texts.
The measurement of thermal expansion of a liquid’s volume or the dimensions of a solid caused by phenomena such as compression and magnetostriction.
Act or process of making thinner, more liquid, or less concentrated, as by admixture of water.
Indicating on a drawing the actual dimensions of whatever is represented in the drawing.
Axonometric projection in which all three spatial axes are inclined, but only two are equally inclined to the plane of projection or drawing surface.
Process whereby the metals to be joined are dipped into a bath of molten salt, which acts as both flux and a source of heat.

Sculptural process in which the sculptor forms the image by cutting away from the block of stone, generally without working from a model. The concept of the work evolves and develops during the carving process and adaptation may be made according to the character of the stone found within the block.
Lighting techniques in which 90 to 100 percent of the light is directed toward the surface to be illuminated; usually downward light.
Processes in which a positive-image photograph is formed in the camera without an intermediate negative, or for positive-to-positive processes.

A form of pyrolysis mass spectrometry in which the temperature of the sample is raised gradually to separate compounds in the evaporation phase from those in the crosslinked fraction of the pyrolysis phase. It is used to provide qualitative views of the broad-band molecular-level characteristics of complex organic samples.
Lighting of an object or work plane primarily from a specific and preferred direction.
The process of taking apart, whether by damage or intentionally; examples are when folios of manuscripts are cut out of the volume for redistribution separately.

The process of removing sections of gathered matter from within a binding with the intention that they stand alone thereafter. Where the gathered matter has been removed from the binding in preparation for rebinding in a different form, use "pulling (dismantling)." Where the gathered matter has been collated but never bound, for example a book in sheets, signatures, or gatherings, use "unbound."

A numerical method used in computing the stresses and displacements of a large number of small particles such as grains of sand. Often used in combination with the finite element method to address engineering questions involving granular and discontinuous materials in continuous physical systems.
Freeing from infection especially by destroying harmful microorganisms.
Dissolution into separate parts, losing intactness or solidness.
Taking apart or removing significant pieces, especially to reduce or disable the function.
Refers to the process of dividing into parts so as to destroy the integrity of a whole, for example, the separation limbs from a body.

Describes the process of creating or transforming objects, disciplines, locales, or elements of culture according to aspects of the Walt Disney Company's theme parks, merchandise, animation style, or public image. Characteristics include an emphasis on escapism, fantasy, consumerism, virtue, and idealized notions of society.
The action of diffusing or spreading, as in particulate matter in liquid suspension.
Refers to the act of dissolving or the condition of being dissolved. In chemistry, molecular dispersion of a substance in a liquid, the transfer of a solid into a solution.

Visual effect in motion pictures in which one scene fades in to another. Both shots are seen briefly superimposed. The dissolve was first used to imply geographic or temporal transitions. Later, the optical nature of the dissolve was used for more tonal effects, such a character's state of mind, fantasy, or flashback sequences.

The volatilization or evaporation and subsequent condensation of a liquid, as when water is boiled and the steam is condensed, for such purposes as purification, concentration, or separation of substances.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: Refers to the general technique of separation of color using marks of any kind in painting or printmaking, particularly in the works of Italian artists. The term was invented by Paul Signac to describe the technique of Neo-Impressionist separation of color into dots or patches applied directly to the canvas, intended to produce maximum brilliance, avoiding muddiness caused by physically mixing colors. While divisionism may be used as a synonym for "pointillism," in modern usage the term "pointillism" often refers specifically to the technique using dots or very short dashes in a more controlled manner.

An approach to photography in which the subject is recorded with an emphasis on factual accuracy and with a high degree of objectivity by the photographer; often of a newsworthy event. May be intended to record, and ultimately influence, social conditions.
Printing techniques in which light is obstructed or reduced in selective portions of the image, lessening exposure (and resulting density) in those areas.
The art or craft of fabricating dolls.
The action of drawing or sketching casually or aimlessly, often while engaged in some other activity such as conversation.

The process or method of measuring the dosage of ionizing radiation received over a given period, whether by individuals or materials and whether to assess a health hazard or to date archaeological materials.

Printmaking process for making relief engravings on metal, called dotted prints, using dots from punches of various shapes and sometimes including burin lines; common in the second half of the 15th century. For later, intaglio processes employing directly engraved flicks or dots for tonal areas, use "stipple engraving."

Adhesive binding method in which loose leaves are fanned in one direction then passed over a glue roller, so that the adhesive penetrates between the leaves; the leaves are then fanned in the other direction and passed over the glue roller again, so that both sides of the binding edge of each leaf are coated and adhere to the adjoining leaves.

The technique of painting two separate images on the fore-edge of a book, one done with the leaves fanned one way, the other with the leaves fanned the other way, so that the two images are visible independently.
Close nailing in which the nails are set in two parallel rows.
Process of reprinting a plate or block to ensure a strong, fully printed image.

Type of weave, primarily used in basketweaving, that consists of alternating splints of plant fiber. Starting at the base, the splints construct one full basket before bending downward to form another basket on the outside of the first.
Tapestry weaving technique in which the weft threads of two adjacent areas are turned alternately around a common end in groups of two or more.
Fastening together two pieces of wood by means of dowels.

Refers to the technique of detecting underground water, minerals, treasure, archaeological remains, and even dead bodies by feeling the motion of a divining rod. A Y-shaped twig is the traditional divining rod, its two prongs twisting or dipping in the hand of the dowser to indicate the nearby presence of the material sought; alternatively metal rods are held parallel a few inches apart and swing across each other. The nature of the stimuli causing these reactions is unexplained, but most dowsers deny that it is a matter of divination.

The technique that produces graphical representations of structures, machines, and their component parts that communicate the engineering intent of a technical design to the craftsman or worker who makes the product. Drafting is based on the concept of orthographic projection, which in turn is the principal concern of the branch of mathematics called descriptive geometry.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: A technique of stroking paint lightly over a rough surface so that it covers the high spots and leaves the depressions untouched, thus creating a broken area of color with irregular spots of the color underneath showing through.
Producing visible forms primarily by delineation, usually by the direct application of material or instrument to the surface of the support.
The stretching, spreading, or shaping of metal by passing through dies or by stamping successively, as with a series of dies or by hammering.
Methods employed in the act of drawing, such as contouring, cross-hatching, or stumping.
Digging or pulling out with a dredge, especially for the purpose of deepening a body of water, searching for foreign objects in a body of water, or for keeping waterways clean.
Removing lime from and acidifying unhaired skins or hides by immersion in a water infusion of fermented bran.
Processes that follow the tanning or tawing of hide or skin, or the stretching and drying of skin that is to be made into parchment, in order to modify it to meet specific needs.
Activity of cutting, sewing, and creating ladies clothing, particularly dresses, coats, and shirts.
Refers to the making of holes in metal, wood, or other materials, and for making holes in the earth, as for drilling a well or for oil.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: Technique of creating paintings by dripping or pouring paint in a semi-controlled manner onto the support.

Tumbling skins, hides, or leather in a revolving container either to remove some substance from them such as water or other liquids, to add a substance to them, such as dye or tanning substances, or to wash them.
Any of various nonaqueous cleaning methods such as the removal of surface dirt by brushing, using appropriate erasers on paper, or the use of nonaqueous organic solvents on fabrics.

A type of collodion process in which the binder was dried before exposure or contained a hygroscopic agent to retain sufficient moisture to allow processing after an extended period of time; an improvement on the wet collodion process because it alleviated the need for a portable darkroom. Used to produce negatives and lantern slides.
Attaching a drawing, print, photograph, or other work of art done on paper to a cardboard or other backing by using a thermoplastic tissue as an adhesive.
The technique of scratching designs or images into a dry plaster surface which has been covered with a thin or moderately thick lime wash layer.
Method of construction where stones are laid or stacked without any mortar to bind them together; stones are typically secured by having carefully selecting interlocking stones.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: The technique of creating a broken or mottled effect with paint or ink on a relatively dry brush, allowing traces of the paper or underpainting to show through.
Curing skins and hides for later tanning by coating them with salt after flaying and hanging them to dry.
Making free or nearly free of liquids. For the removal or deprivation of water, though other liquids may remain, use "dehydration."
Intaglio process in which a sharp needle scratches the plate creating a burr that yields a characteristically soft and velvety line in the final print.
Refers to a construction technique that employs dry materials such as wallboard, plywood, or other prefabricated materials, without the use of plaster or mortar.
Copying process that uses visible light from one band of the electromagnetic spectrum to form a latent image and infrared radiation to make the image visible on the copy paper.
The process of gilding the edges of a book with a piece of paper laid over the gold and the burnisher rubbed over the paper, resulting in a dull finish.
In ceramics, cracking that occurs during firing or cooling due to a rapid changes in temperature; can occur in bisquits or glazed ware.

Photographic process in which the image is formed by dusting a pigment onto a surface of bichromated colloid that has been exposed through a transparency. Areas exposed to light lose their tackiness and hold less pigment. Generally for prints on glass or ceramic, where the pigment is fired as a glaze.
Process by which a continuous tone digital image is applied to receiver materials via thermal transfer of cyan, yellow, and magenta dyes.
Diffusion transfer process that uses dye couplers rather than silver and produces color instant camera photographs.

Photomechanical process in which gelatin relief matrices are made by a bichromate process from color separation negatives or positives; a full-color print is then made by transferring dye from the matrices, in register, to a support.
Process of applying a coloring matter that is in a solution or fine dispersion so that at least some of the coloring matter is taken up by the substance.
Analysis under dynamic conditions.

A form of thermal analysis rooted in the science of rheology, a branch of mechanics that deals with the flow deformation of materials in terms of force (stress, strain) and time. DMA is a technique in which rheological data for a particular material is generated using a rheometer and software. The material is characterized in terms of its modulus, elasticity, viscosity, damping behavior, and glass transition temperature, and the changes of these with strain, strain rate, temperature, and oscillatory frequency.

Process or technique of constructing architecture or other works using earth, either by digging into the earth or by creating building materials from earth. If the earth has been fired, such as with bricks, prefer "masonry construction."

The activity of painting relatively small paintings, originally using an easel or other device in the process. The term is used to refer to the process used by artists in the west, dating from the 16th century and later, when art was considered valuable in its own right, and to distinguish paintings on panel and canvas from mural paintings, manuscript illuminations, or decorative painting on objects.

Process introduced by the Eastman Kodak Co. in the early 1930s for printing color images from gelatin matrices which are prepared by exposure to separation negatives. The process is seen as a prototype for the modern-day dye transfer process.
In bookbinding, the process of gilding, coloring, tooling, painting, printing, or other by other means coating or marking one or more edges of a book.
The binding of identical books in quantity, using high-speed automatic equipment, usually for a publisher or distributor and usually case binding in hard covers.

A subtractive color reversal process using a film with an integral tripack, each layer containing dyes appropriate to the color sensitivity of the layer. The image is formed by the chromogenic process.

Method of rock fragmentation which employs pulsed power in short, high power bursts of electrical energy to create high internal tensile stresses which fracture materials along grain boundaries. Electric pulse disaggregation (EPD) is an efficient method for separating a rock's constituents with applications that include separation of single crystals for isotopic measurement, and fossil preparation.
Any of a number of methods of welding that use electricity as a source of power.

A geophysical technique of surveying involving the measurement of lateral and vertical variations in resistivity of the earth to an electric current passing between metal electrodes; used in archaeology for 3D reconstruction of the subsurface.

Application of an electric DC field for removal of the solubilized salt ions by electromigration. During the process, the concentrations of ions of salts will decrease as these ions concentrate around an electrode of opposite polarity. The electrodes are placed in or on the surface or on a poultice on a salt-infected material and the ions concentrate here. After the desalination the poultice is removed and thereby, the damaging ions.
Corrosion that occurs when current flows between cathodic and anodic areas on metallic surfaces.

Technique that quantitatively measures the amount of electrochemical corrosion resistance provided by a coating to a metal substrate. In conservation science it is used extensively for evaluating the anticorrosion performance of coatings to metal works of art.
Size exclusion chromatography technique that uses electrophoresis to resolve and separate large biomolecules; also known as electropherography.

The process by which a decorative or protective coating of any material is deposited onto metal by means of an electrolytic bath. Examples include electroplating which is the electrodeposition of metal; and electrodeposition of paint.
Dialysis that is accelerated by an electromotive force which is applied to electrodes adjacent to the membranes.

The technique of making an exact facsimile of a model, sometimes a wax model but often an actual article of which reproductions are sought, by depositing a thin layer of a metal, such as copper or silver, by means of an electric current.
Galvanizing by a process in which the zinc is deposited by an electroplating method.
Generic term for the class of electrostatic copying techniques.
Deposition of a metallic coating by a controlled chemical reduction that is catalyzed by the metal or alloy being deposited.

The passage of an electric current through an electrolyte with subsequent migration of positively and negatively charged ions to the negative and positive electrodes. In conservation used for cleaning off surface deposits.
Creating marks on the surface of a hard material, usually metal, by electrolysis.
Process for producing paper copies of microform originals, depending on electrolytic image formation on a special paper having metallic layers.
Migration of ions in an electric field under the influence of an electrical potential difference.

Application of electron diffraction in the backscattering mode as used in analysis of crystallographic structure of textured materials that show a preferred orientation of crystalline components. Electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) is based on the measurement of the diffraction pattern formed by the electrons backscattered toward a phosphor screen positioned at an angle from the sample surface. The visible lines generated by the impinging electrons are called Kikuchi bands; thus the technique is also known as backscatter Kikuchi diffraction (BKD).
Treatment of objects under elevated temperature and nitrogen atmosphere by high energy electrons for various purposes including cross-linking of polymers and sterilization.

Electron energy loss spectroscopy measures the energy of scattered electrons reemitted from surfaces after bombardment by an electron beam, thus determining the distribution of energy loss. Provides data complementary to Auger electron spectroscopy which operates according to the same principle but uses a different process to produce the electrons.
The use of or investigation with an electron microscope.
Technique in microanalytical chemistry in which a finely focused electron beam is used to excite an x-ray spectrum.
A radiographic technique that uses secondary electrons emitted from a thin lead foil (100 to 250 micrometers) to produce an image of the examined object on a radiographic film.

The process of recording, transferring, storage, and manipulation of graphic information (such as maps) or images (such as art works) into electronic format for storage, retrieval, and use in computer and video systems.

Refers to an optical technique which enables interferometric measurements of surface displacements. When laser light is scattered by a rough surface it generates a random intensity distribution known as a speckle pattern.The technique allows visualization and measurement of the three dimensional distribution of displacement and strain/stress on the object.
Movement of molecules of a liquid through a semipermeable biological membrane from a less concentrated field into one of greater solute concentration under the influence of an electric field.

The movement of charged, heterogeneous particles in a suspension gel under the influence of an electric field created by electrodes of opposite charge set on either side of the suspension; used as technique for analysis of macromolecular substances such as DNA and proteins.
Creation of images by alteration to the electrical properties of the senstitive material as a result of the action of light.
Applying a protective or decorative coating of one metal to another by means of an electrolytic bath.
Producing a smooth bright surface on a metal object by immersion as an anode in an electrolytic bath.

Technique developed by John Fenn in the late 1980s, an improved method of ionizing samples for use in mass spectrometry. Electrospray ionization (ESI) discharges a solution of a sample into an electric field that disperses the sample as fine droplets. ESI is especially useful for samples containing macromolecules, prone to fragmenting in other techniques of ionization.

A machine-assisted treatment method of removing dust and biological contaminants from paper, photographic materials, and textiles. Contaminant removal is performed by frictionless application of an electrostatic foil. The technology allows removal of dust contaminants from both sides, even from delicate surfaces, while keeping existing drawing and writing materials or pigment layers intact. In the cleaning and hygiene industry, the term is often used interchangeably with electrostatic disinfecting, which sprays a negatively-charged disinfecting solution onto a contaminated surface.
The process of making printing plates by using a mold of another printing surface, such as a wood engraving or set type, and electroplating with copper.
Analysis  of a sample of some material by any means to determine and quantify its elemental composition.
Notations indicating on a drawing the elevation of each point indicated by a datum mark.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: A method of painting in oils underwater developed in the 18th century by Arnaud Vincent de Montpetit.  Montpetit believed that this technique of painting would preserve a work and eliminate the need for restoration in the future.
The process of separating out finer particles in a mixture from courser ones by washing or decanting; often used in ceramics to create more finely grained clay body.
The process of treating a dead body to prevent decay.
The condition of being firmly fixed in a surrounding mass of some solid material.
Producing raised letters or designs on a surface, typically by means of stamping or molding.
Process of becoming brittle; includes the loss of flexibilty or elasticity.

Refers to the process in which a needle and thread or fine wire are used to stitch decorative designs into cloth, leather, paper, or other material. It may also refer to the process used to create machine-made imitations of hand-made embroidery.  For the weft patterning technique of weaving raised patterns on a woven textile, use "brocading."

In emission spectroscopy, use of an energetic medium promotes the excitation of atoms in a sample. The resulting spectrum of electromagnetic radiation emitted is monitored and analysed using a spectrometer. The most important variant of the technique is atomic emission spectroscopy.
Enamel decoration characterized by an opaque coating of enamel.
The process of applying a vitreous coating to metal, ceramic, glass, or other surfaces by fusion using heat in a kiln or furnace, with the result of creating a smooth, hard surface.
Surrounding, enclosing, or protecting something as if in a capsule; for example, in paper conservation, the sealing of an item between two sheets of polyester film.

Technique involving the working of the surface of certain areas of a painting or polychromed carved figure to imitate the color and shading of human flesh; usually referring to Caucasian flesh color. The term was first coined in reference to Spanish art, but may be applied to any western art; particularly in use prior to the early 1900s. In some cases, certain artists specialized in encarnacione, while other artists would paint drapery, landscape, and other areas of the work.

The technique of decorating clay in which the body material is inlaid with the clay of another color. For the technique of wall or panel painting using pigment suspendied in molten wax, use "encaustic painting."

Technique of painting with pigments dispersed in molten wax. It is applied while warm to panels or murals with a brush or palette, and fixed by passing a heat source over the surface to fuse and permanently bond the paint. It is an ancient technique that was particularly popular in ancient Greece. The term derives from a Greek word meaning 'burnt in.' Although it fell into disuse in the 8th or 9th centuries C.E., there continue to be exponents of the technique today. For the technique of decorating clay, use "encaustic decoration."
Decorative technique for the application of precious materials like gold to the surfaces of objects, or the application of marble or other materials to the surfaces of built works.

In medicine, direct examination of the interior parts of the body by an optical instrument introduced through insertion or ingestion, the endoscope; in conservation, endoscopy may be performed on objects with hollow bodies, such as musical instruments, globes, or mummies.

Qualitative and quantitative element analysis made by applying X-ray to the sample and then analysing the re-emitted element characteristic fluorescent X-ray. Examples of use are in chemical, electronic, food, and refinery industries.
The technique of engraving and decorating metal with an overall pattern of continuous, encircling, and contiguous narrow grooves, usually of a wavy character.
Technique of bond in which alternating courses of all headers and all stretchers are laid, with the headers centered on the stretchers and on the joints between stretchers.

Creating marks on the surface of a hard material, such as metal or glass, by incising with a sharp tool, when the designs or images created are for their own sake and not to be printed from. For printing processes that involve this technique plus intaglio printing from the surface, use "engraving (printing process)."

The intaglio process in which the design is incised into a printing plate, usually a flat copper plate, with the aid of a graver or burin that is held in the palm of the hand and pushed against the copper to cut lines comprising V-shaped grooves. The plate is then inked up, wiped so that ink is retained in the grooves and then forced out under the pressure of the printing process to create lines on the paper. The technique was first developed in the early 15th century in Germany, probably by goldsmiths who wished to keep records of the designs they had engraved on their wares. The process is distinct from "wood engraving (process)," which is a process for relief printing; "wood cut (process)" refers to engraving wood blocks for printing. Historically, "engraving" has sometimes been used incorrectly to refer to all printmaking processes, particulalry any process employing printing plates. For the single step of incising an inscription or design into any surface, not only a printing plate, see "engraving (incising)."
Refers to the darkroom procedure in which a photographic image is magnified by being projected through an optical system; generally used for making prints from small negatives.

Form of scanning electron microscopy that provides electron imaging at a relatively high degree of sample pressure. It allows the option of capturing photomicrographs of wet or uncoated specimens by offering a gaseous environment within the specimen chamber.
Assay technique in which a monoclonal antibody is bound to an enzyme to detect and measure the presence of an antigen in a sample.

Refers to the most commonly used flourescence microscopy technique. Exciting radiation is mounted above ('epi') the specimen and is reflected downwards, passing through the microscope objective lens on its way toward the specimen.
Refers to a system of projection generally used in map-making, where distances are true to scale in all directions when taken from a given point or along a given meridian or parallel.
Refers to the action of effacing, rubbing out, or removing something written, drawn, engraved, or recorded on any media.
Process whereby materials are worn away and removed by natural causes including weathering, solution, corrosion, or transportation.
Technique used in Spanish painting which involves working a painted surface to create a pattern; used to embellish the robes of carved and painted figures.
Refers to various photomechanical processes that use a daguerreotype to make the printing plate.

Creating marks on the surface of a hard material such as metal or glass, by the controlled corrosive action of acid. Use "etching (printing process)" for the series of steps that includes printing from a bitten plate.

Intaglio process in which the design is worked into an acid-resistant substance coating the metal printing plate; the plate is then exposed to acid, which etches the plate where the metal is exposed, to create lines and dark areas. For the single step of exposing the plate to acid, use "biting."

A diagnostic method based on electromagnetic measurement of the permittivity of the material under investigation, such as brick, plaster, and stone. The theoretical concept is based on the measurement of the dielectric contrast between water and the host material in the microwave frequency range, and is successful up to 2 cm in depth. In conservation, this method has been usefully employed for determining by non-invasiveway the moisture and salts content inside materials.
The process by which any substance is converted from a liquid state into a vapor or gaseous state.

Evolved gas analysis (EGA) is a technique whereby temperature-resolved, volatile species generated from a sample placed within a microfurnace are analyzed using mass spectrometry. It is the most useful technique to determine the nature and amount of volatile products formed during the thermal degradation of materials.
Generally, the action or process of digging out a hollow or hollows in the earth. Specifically, refers to the systematic digging and documentation of an archaeological site.
the process of a whole object separating itself into layers, scales, or laminae.
The act or activity of making photographic images that document the installation of an exhibition in a museum, gallery, or other venue.
The act of digging up or otherwise removing a body from a grave, disinterment.
The increase in length or volume of a material, or a body, caused by temperature, moisture, or other environmental condition.
A technique of building where all or part of the frame, usually wooden or metal, is exposed on the exterior rather than being hidden by walls.
The act of presenting a photosensitive surface to actinic rays or radiant energy, especially light; and the extent of radiant energy received in this context.
The process of augmenting the contents of an individual book by adding prints, engravings, letters, or other documents after publication, often resulting in its rebinding.
Signifies the act of drawing forth a material from that which holds it.
The process of shaping a heated or unheated material, such as plastic or metal, by forcing or pressing it through a dye or other shaping device.

Construction technique where the facade of a building is designed or constructed separately from the rest of a building, or where the front elevation of an older building is preserved with a new building constructed behind it.
A process in which a print, usually a photograph, is permanently adhered to a clear sheet of acrylic. This enhances the perception of color and sharpness, due to the acrylic's refractive qualities.
Shaping something, as a gem, glass, or ceramics, so that it has a series of relatively small, regular, flat surfaces that reflect light.
The process of covering the front surface of something with new material.
Process of copying written or printed text or images by radio or telephone transmission for exact reproduction elsewhere.
The process, manner, or style in which a thing is made, including the materials with which it is made and workmanship.

A transitional visual effect used to indicate to beginning of one scene and the ending of another by means of an image gradually disappearing (fade-out), while another image grows stronger and gradually comes into view (fade-in). An image fading into black typically indicates a major separation of action, while a fade to white can indicate a character's loss of consciousness or death.
A gradual loss of color or intensity.

Quilting technique in which layers of cloth with no filling between them are back-stitched together under sufficient tension so as to raise the ground very slightly, thus giving the impression of filling.

Infrared photography technique commonly applied to a variety of techniques including aerial photography and art examination. False color infrared is preferred for vegetative and hydrologic studies. False color infrared film is sensitive to ultraviolet, visible and infrared radiation to approximately 900 nm. When the film is exposed with a yellow filter, the emulsion layers become sensitive to only green, red and infrared wavelengths. Blue radiation is absorbed by the filter; hence, the greater haze penetration ability of infrared film. In art conservation, the resulting false color images can be used as a noninvasive means of identifying the pigments that have been used in a painting or underdrawing.

Refers to hand sewing characterized by techniques more elaborate than used in plain sewing. It includes embroidery, smocking, and quilting, and other works done with a needle and thread. The purpose of fancy sewing is generally to create decoration rather than to bind pieces of material together.
Infrared spectroscopy having the range of 10-400 inverse centimeters [cm-1].
The activity of drawing or painting illustrations of fashionable apparel and accessories, especially for advertisement.
Photography of fashionable apparel and accessories, especially for advertisement.

Visual effect that occurs when film is advanced at less than twenty-four frames per second, so that when the film is projected at standard speed the action appears accelerated. Most silent films were shot around 16 frames per second, thus the motion appears fast, and sometimes this herky-jerky motion is associated with them. Fast motion was a favorite technique of director Mack Sennet, who used it in silent comedies and it has since been used in more modern pictures such as Tony Richardson's "Tom Jones" (1963).
Architectural project management technique whereby construction is allowed to begin before the design and specifications are completed.
The action of attaching something firmly or securely to something else with a bond of any kind.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: The practice of applying layers of color rich in oil over an underpainting that contains less oil and is therefore less flexible.

Filling the fibers of light leather, such as that used for gloves, with an emulsion of oils or grease and water to make the fibers water-repellent and flexible; distinguished from "currying," which is introducing fatty substances into heavy leather.

Term used to describe a wide range of decorative painting techniques originally intended to replicate materials such as marble and wood with paint. Usage encompasses many other decorative finishes for walls and furniture, and is used broadly to describe various techniques in the context of domestic decor. For illusionistic painting in the context of fine art use "trompe l'oeil."
The process of making works primarily of feathers; usually feathers are either adhered to a solid support or tied individually to a woven support.
Applying heat, pressure, and moisture to cause fiber to adhere and mat together. For the controlled shrinking of textile in order to make it thicker and more compact, use "fulling."
Chinese site analysis method that employs geomantic techniques; used especially in determining the placement and design of houses, graves, and gardens.
An iron process largely used for reproducing technical drawings; produces an image in black. The image is developed out with gallic acid and is fixed by washing in water.
Photographic processing procedure in which a print having a gelatin emulsion layer is dried in contact with a polished surface so that the gelatin retains a highly glazed surface.
The action or process of conveying or transporting over water, usually a small body of water.
Preparing a clay object for firing by trimming away seams and other blemishes with a metal tool; especially used upon clay objects formed with molds.
The processing of flax, hemp, cotton, or another fiber by separating the fine fibers from the impurities such as the seeds, straw, and woody stems.
Scanning electron microscopy performed using a field-emission cathode in the instrument's electron gun, producing clearer, less electrostatically distorted images than conventional SEM.
The action of shaving, forming, or reducing something with a hardened, usually metallic, tool with cutting ridges.
Supplementary lighting to reduce, soften, or eliminate shadows caused by the main source of illumination.
Inserting material into a hole, crack, or cavity. In the specific context of mosaic conservation, the application of mortar or other material into an area of loss of the mosaic surface.

Collectively, all the processes and techniques employed in the creation of motion pictures on film or videotape, including directing, performing, producing, filming, editing, writing and design. Specifically refers to the activities of film or video directors and producers.
Effects, usually pre-set algorithms, that are applied to a digital image to create variations.

The process of passing a liquid or other material through a filter or porous medium in order to remove  or separate solid particles or impurities. May also refer to the process of passing an electrical signal or other signal through a filter to remove excess noise or undesired frequencies.
The practice and activity of woodworking that results in the creation of  fine art or otherwise exhibits craftmanship of particularly high quality.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: Technique achieved by applying paint using the digits of the hand rather than a brush. Examples range from prehistoric image-making, modern art-making techniques, and children's art.

Process of weaving using one's hands without the use of a loom, joining lengthwise and sideways threads together. One end of the strands are pinned to a stable surface by a bar or nail. Primarily practiced by Native North Americans.

Refers generally to processes that complete an object, treatment, or work, including the chemical, electrolytic, and mechanical application of finishes. In the context of bookbinding, refers to that sequence of processes carried out on a book and its binding after forwarding, up to the point of attaching its cover. In case binding, where the cover is attached prior to the application of lettering or decoration, the attachment of the cover is part of the forwarding, not the finishing. In the context of leather manufacture, all the processes carried out on the leather after tanning. In the context of papermaking, all the processes carried out on the paper after it leaves the papermaking machine.
An approximation method for analyzing continuous physical systems, used in structural mechanics, electrical field theory, and fluid mechanics.
The process of reheating glassware in order to impart a smooth or brilliant surface.
Method of construction that prevents or retards the passage of hot gases or flames.
Refers to the processes of preparing, curing, or purposely transforming materials, such as clay, silica, or glass, by means of heat.
Method of dating glassy materials or objects based on the known rate of spontaneous fission of uranium 233 atoms, which leave detectable damage trails, called fission tracks.
Making a material or object firm, stable, or stationary.
Separating into thin flattened pieces or layers.
The technique of taking photographs using a brief, brilliant burst of light, synchronized with the camera's shutter activation.

Experimental technique used to analyze short-lived chemical intermediates created when molecules are split by an intense flash of light. Analysis by spectrophotometry follows during a second, less intense, flash. This technique is useful in the study of the mechanisms of fast chemical reactions.
A non-destructive testing method that is a form of themographic inspection involving imaging of the thermal patterns on a sample surface heated by a brief pulse of light.
A method of coloring glass by dipping clear glass, while still pliant, into a bath of molten glass of a deep color, or by fusing a thin piece of dark glass to a thicker one of clear glass.
A type of chasing done to decorate a flat surface rather than to develop relief decoration.
Technique which involves the application of flat color over an area too large to be covered by a single brush stroke.
A minimally non-destructive technique used to assess in situ the mechanical properties of masonry walls and the local average compressive stress.
Weaving process in which no pile, hand-knotting, or hand-tufting is used, typically with reference to rugs, towels, or other textiles that typically may be expected to have a pile.
Making flat, as in removing wrinkles or folds.
Technique of bond in which headers and stretchers alternate horizontally and vertically, each header being centered with regard to the stretcher above and below.
The cleaning and leveling of the flesh side of hides and skins by the removal of inner membranes such as hypodermal and muscle tissue in preparation for further processing.
Type of mounting in which an artwork shows the edges of the paper within the window mat, and appears to float above the backing board.
Surface finishing process for concrete and mortar that embeds aggregate, removes slight imperfections, humps, and voids to produce a level surface, and consolidates material rising to the surface.

Perspective used in certain landscapes in Chinese painting, characterized by having not only one point of view, but including several perspectives, often shifting from near to far vignettes. The philosophy of this perspective scheme is to achieve an "angle of totality," by which the viewer can see all parts of the scene to best advantage.
Creating a textured effect similar to suede or felt by blowing fibers of cotton, silk, nylon, or other material onto a tacky surface, usually paper.

A technology that provides rapid multi-parametric analysis of single cells in solution. Flow cytometers utilize lasers as light sources to produce both scattered and fluorescent light signals. Cell populations can be analyzed and/or purified based on their fluorescent or light scattering characteristics.
Removing defects from the flesh side of leather by means of an abrasive or abrading tool.  For the removal of defects from the grain side of leather by similar means, use "buffing (leather process)."
The use of or investigation with a fluorescence microscope.
Painting technique that explores luminosity through the use of fluorescent paints which reflect absorbed ultraviolet energy as visible light.
Measuring of fluorescence with a fluorimeter; a technique that stimulates substances with monochromatic radiation causing them to emit fluorescence.
Method of relative dating by measuring the amount of fluorine absorbed by groundwater percolation into the crystal lattice of the mineral component of buried bone.

A dynamic x-ray imaging technique used to examine the internal structure of an object, fluoroscopy produces real time video images by converting x-rays into light (via a fluorescent screen) which is captured by a TV camera. Fluoroscopy is used primarily for medical imaging, but also in the examination of archaeological finds and works of art.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
scopeNote_en: Chinese painting technique in which the brush is applied quickly across the paper, which separates the hairs of the brush, leaving streaks of untouched white background, giving the design a light and airy quality. Most frequently used in bamboo and bird and flower paintings.
Application of foil to a surface by use of heat and a die, in order to cover or to create a design or printing on the surface.

Bending something over upon itself, as cloth or paper. For the parts bent over upon each other, use "folds"; for resulting ridges, use "creases." To describe objects designed to fold into a compact form, use "folding (attribute)."
The placing of consecutive numbers one on each folio of a book; may also refer to the overall configuration of the numbering in the book.

The practice of painting on the fore edge of a book, either with the edge solid so that the resulting image is visible with the book closed, or, in the more usual sense of the term, with the edges fanned out so that the image is visible only when the book is open. The edge may also be gilded or marbled in the usual manner.
Photography providing evidence that can be admitted in a court of law and that has been produced according to strict technical requirements.
In pictorial representation, the diminishing of certain dimensions of an object or figure in order to indicate its recession into the pictorial space.
The uniting of two pieces of hot metal by hammering them together on an anvil.
Techniques involving making, imitating, or altering valued objects or documents with intent to deceive. Distinguished from "copying" by the intention of deception.
Forming the shape of a metal object by heating and hammering.
Shaping, molding, or fashioning into a certain state or condition.
Strengthening elements of the built environment against attack, providing them with some means of defense, or protecting them with fortifications or fortification elements.
The sequence of processes involved in binding a book following the sewing or stitching of the text block up to and including covering.

Die Kunst oder der Prozess Fotografien herzustellen. Diese sind Bilder, die mit Hilfe einer chemischen Reaktion von Licht auf einen empfindlichen Filme, empfindliches Glas, Papier oder Metall entstehen.
Process of melting and pouring materials, such as metal or glass, into a mold; for glass also includes fusing.
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy adapted for selective analysis of components of heterogeneous samples on a micrometer scale.

Method of obtaining infrared spectra absorption, emission, photoconductivity or Raman scattering solids, liquids or gas by first collecting an interferogram of a sample signal using an interferometer, and then performing a Fourier Transform (FT) on the interferogram to obtain the spectrum.
Raman spectroscopy which employs Fourier transform-based signal treatment to improve the quality of the measured spectra.
In chemistry, a process of separation in which components of a mixture are removed in successive stages.

Study of fractured surfaces of materials, visually or with a microscope. By determining type of crack growth, fractography aims to identify the mode of failure and the conditions which might cause it.
Providing a border or case to surround, support, enclose, or call attention to such items as a work of art, mirror, or document, leaving the item itself visible.
Marking an object with a postal or other mark to waive the charge for, or otherwise facilitate, its passage.
Refers to a method of drying materials or objects in a frozen state.

Deteriorative weathering mechanism caused by cycle of fluctuation in temperature below and above the freezing point of water. At temperatures above freezing, liquid water seeps into cracks, expanding within them in the form of ice as temperature falls below the freezing point. Repeated freeze-thaw cycles may lead to deterioration of stone and such effects as spalling in masonry, and potholes in roads.
Causing to become hardened into a solid body by the abstraction of heat.

A process of plating copper, also sometimes brass or iron, with silver leaf, as used by French platers from the early 18th century, and later by English platers, to repair imperfectly plated copper pieces or to refinish exposed copper on worn pieces. Similar to "close plating"  but costlier.
Machine or hand sewing of text blocks without the use of tapes, cords, or other sewing supports.

parent_en: <painting techniques by application method or circumstances>
